ops.title: In jj-colocated repos, never git stash or worktree+cherry-pick for dirty-tree rebase/sync — the dirty tree is just @, so jj git fetch + jj rebase handles it
ops.body:
In a colocated `jj` repo, the dirty working tree is just the working-copy commit `@` — jj auto-snapshots it, so `jj rebase` needs no clean tree. To land a commit on an updated remote: `jj git fetch` then `jj rebase -r <change> -o main@origin` (use `-s` instead of `-r` if `@` is a descendant that should ride on top), then `jj bookmark set main -r <rebased>` and `jj git push --bookmark main`. The in-flight `@` is never touched. Never reach for `git stash`, and never do a git-worktree + cherry-pick dance.

**Why:** User feedback (2026-06-18): an agent in the cc-notes repo rebased a feature commit onto an updated `origin/main` via a worktree + cherry-pick and left local `main` diverged, "instead of just using jj." Agents keep applying git's needs-a-clean-tree mental model in jj repos where it doesn't apply.

**How to apply:** In any jj repo, when the tree is dirty and you need to rebase/sync onto an updated remote, reach for `jj git fetch` + `jj rebase` over the named revision (`-r`), not git stash/worktree gymnastics. The destination flag is `-d`/`--onto` — **both work in current jj** (2026-07: `jj rebase -r <c> -d <dest>` succeeded; the older "there is no -d, only -o" claim is stale). Pick `-r` for a lone commit whose siblings/`@` must stay put, `-s` when descendants should follow, never `-b` (it sweeps unrelated commits in).

**Operational lessons (2026-07 fleet sweep):** (1) To land an isolated commit touching only some paths while a repo's `@` holds unrelated WIP, use **`jj commit <paths> -m MSG`** — it commits just those paths into a new commit and the remaining WIP rides in the new `@` on top; then `jj bookmark set main -r @-` + push. This preserved active WIP across ~16 jj repos while pushing only workflow edits. (2) **`jj git fetch` has NO `-q`/`--quiet` flag** — passing it errors and (under `2>/dev/null`) silently does nothing, leaving `main@origin` stale; never script it with `-q`. (3) The **push's own "references unexpectedly moved on the remote (stale info)" check is the real gate** against a concurrent origin advance — recover with `jj git fetch` + `jj rebase -r <mine> -d main@origin` + re-set bookmark + push (watch for a `main` bookmark-conflict → pick your commit by id). Set the bookmark to the rebased commit and push so local `main` stays fast-forward, not diverged. In **cc-notes** repos, `jj git push`/`fetch` carry only `refs/heads/*` — run `cc-notes sync` to move `refs/cc-notes/*` (see [[jj-snapshot-limit-vs-brand-logos]] for jj's other git-bridge gotcha). Push and verify in the same step ([[always-push-commits]]).

**One sanctioned worktree exception (2026-07-05, captain-hook ship):** when `@` sits on a fork whose version of a file (e.g. CHANGELOG.md) diverged from origin, ANY commit of that file built from `@` 3-way-conflicts on rebase — same-region different insertions, and jj rebase can't auto-merge them. `jj rebase` is the wrong tool there. Instead: strip the file from the fileset commit (`jj squash --from <c> --into @ -- <file>`), rebase the code-only commit cleanly, then author the file's edit in a **throwaway `git worktree add <scratchpad> origin/main`** (edit against origin's content, commit, push, `git worktree remove`) — the shared `@` and the concurrent session's WIP are never touched. Bonus: the worktree doubles as the place to run the full test suite against the *merged* (origin + your commit) tree before pushing, which the fork-based working copy cannot do.
